title: Cash vs Mobile Payment China
metaDescription: Essential guide for tourists navigating cash and mobile payments like WeChat Pay and Alipay while traveling in China.

sections.heading: Getting Started
sections.content: Download WeChat or Alipay before arriving in China — both now support international credit cards. Carry small amounts of cash (100-200 RMB daily) for backup at traditional markets or street vendors. Inform your bank of your travel dates to avoid card blocks when linking to mobile apps.

sections.heading: Key Tips
sections.content: Link an international Visa, Mastercard, or other supported card directly inside the Alipay or WeChat Pay app under the international user option. Street food stalls, small shops, and taxis overwhelmingly prefer mobile payment over cash — having QR code payment ready saves time. Keep some 10 yuan notes handy for small purchases like bottled water at rural areas or older vendors who may not accept digital payments.

sections.heading: What to Remember
sections.content: Mobile payment is nearly universal in Chinese cities, but cash is not completely dead — rural areas and some temples or scenic spots still rely on it. Your foreign credit card may incur small transaction fees on each mobile payment, so check your bank's international fee policy. Always carry your passport, as some payment verification steps or hotel bookings may require it as ID.

keyTakeaways: Set up Alipay or WeChat Pay with an international card before your trip
Carry small bills as backup for rural areas and traditional markets
Mobile payment dominates Chinese cities — embracing it makes travel far smoother
relatedPhrases: paying in China without Chinese bank account
